coredata.dc:title: Visible-NIR light-responsive 0D/2D CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets with enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance of RhB: Unveiling the dual roles of CQDs and mechanism study
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wenxuan
Wang, Zhuangzhuang
Li, Yuan
Ghasemi, Jahan B.
Li, Jun
Zhang, Gaoke
coredata.dc:description: Environmental pollution caused by dye wastewater discharge has attracted much attention in the past decades. Developing photocatalysts with high solar energy utilization efficiency for the treatment of dye wastewater is one of the most promising methods to address afore-mentioned environmental problem. Herein, novel Vis-NIR (visible to near-infrared) light-responsive carbon quantum dots modified Sb2WO6 (CQDs/Sb2WO6) nanosheets with remarkably enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance of Rhodamine B (RhB) aqueous solution were successfully synthesized. Under the irradiation of Vis light, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency reaches 83% over the optimal composite, which is nearly seven times higher than that of pristine Sb2WO6. Meanwhile, under NIR light irradiation, the optimal composite also keeps a stable degradation performance, while pristine Sb2WO6 exhibits sluggish performance. Besides, a detailed photocatalytic degradation pathway was proposed via the analyses of corresponding intermediates in the photocatalytic degradation process. On the basis of electron spin resonance spectrometry, quenching experiment and density functional theory (DFT) calculation, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) play a dominating role in the photocatalytic reactions and a possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism was unearthed. This work provides new insights for constructing novel Vis-NIR responsive photocatalysts to purify dye wastewater for environmental remediation.

head.ce:title: Visible-NIR light-responsive 0D/2D CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets with enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance of RhB: Unveiling the dual roles of CQDs and mechanism study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Environmental pollution caused by dye wastewater discharge has attracted much attention in the past decades. Developing photocatalysts with high solar energy utilization efficiency for the treatment of dye wastewater is one of the most promising methods to address afore-mentioned environmental problem. Herein, novel Vis-NIR (visible to near-infrared) light-responsive carbon quantum dots modified Sb2WO6 (CQDs/Sb2WO6) nanosheets with remarkably enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance of Rhodamine B (RhB) aqueous solution were successfully synthesized. Under the irradiation of Vis light, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency reaches 83% over the optimal composite, which is nearly seven times higher than that of pristine Sb2WO6. Meanwhile, under NIR light irradiation, the optimal composite also keeps a stable degradation performance, while pristine Sb2WO6 exhibits sluggish performance. Besides, a detailed photocatalytic degradation pathway was proposed via the analyses of corresponding intermediates in the photocatalytic degradation process. On the basis of electron spin resonance spectrometry, quenching experiment and density functional theory (DFT) calculation, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) play a dominating role in the photocatalytic reactions and a possible photocatalytic degradation mechanism was unearthed. This work provides new insights for constructing novel Vis-NIR responsive photocatalysts to purify dye wastewater for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: The problem of water environment deterioration caused by large amount of industrial wastewater discharge has been widely concerned, especially dye wastewater has become one of the industrial wastewater which is difficult to treat because of its large discharge, poor biodegradability and difficult degradation. Some traditional sewage treatment methods still have some problems, such as low efficiency, high cost and complexity (Pandiyan et al., 2022; Tran et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021b). Photocatalytic technology, as one of the most promising advanced oxidation techniques, has played a pivotal role in wastewater purification for its robust redox ability, great stability and green sustainability (Anwer et al., 2019; Bilal Tahir and Sagir, 2019; Natarajan et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2021). To date, large amounts of novel photocatalysts have been developed to exert their potential at ultraviolet or visible light regions (Ding et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2021; Hu et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2021a,b). However, these kinds of studies about the near infrared (NIR) light (accounting for 50% of the solar energy) driven photocatalysis for wastewater purification are rare (Tian et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021). In terms of the energy utilization, it is of great urgency and significance indeed to exploit NIR responsive photocatalyst to broaden the utilization efficiency of solar light for the treatment of wastewater. Unfortunately, the photocatalysts which can directly harvest NIR light usually possess extremely narrow band gap values, that makes the photogenerated carriers recombine easily, and it difficult to induce the generation of activating free radicals for efficient degradation of organic pollutants. In view of this point, researchers have designed many methods, such as doping，triggering vacancies and coupling for modifying an original non-NIR responsive photocatalyst to indirectly endow it with an efficient NIR light driven photocatalytic performance (Jiang et al., 2021a; Li et al., 2019b; Liang et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020). Among these methods, coupling a substrate photocatalyst with an up-converted material is easy to control. Therefore, finding a reasonable up-converted material to couple with an ultraviolet (UV) or visible light-responsive photocatalyst to indirectly make it with a stable NIR light-responsive catalytic activity is meaningful.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as an emerging class of quasi-spherical carbon nanomaterials with a diameter less than 10 nm, are increasingly applied into the photocatalytic field due to their non-toxic, biocompatible and great water-soluble properties (Wang et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2020). More importantly, some specific CQDs possess an outstanding up-converted property, which makes them a great candidate to couple with other photocatalysts to develop novel NIR light-responsive photocatalysts. Besides, CQDs can usually serve as an electron sink, fostering the separation of photoinduced carriers originating from the substrate photocatalyst. To date, many works about CQDs-based photocatalytic composites have been reported with NIR light-responsive catalytic performance, such as CQDs/H-TiO2 (Tian et al., 2015), CQDs/ZnIn2S4/BiOCl (Jiang et al., 2019), BiVO4/Ag@N-CQDs (Zhang et al., 2021a),and ZIS/CQDs (Xu et al., 2018). However, among these works, the significant roles of CQDs in the composites were scarcely discussed from the atomic level. In this case, it is essential to introduce the up-converted CQDs on the surface of another photocatalyst to deeply study its roles either from a mechanism aspect or from simulation calculation. Antimony tungstate (Sb2WO6), as a typical visible light-responsive photocatalyst, has a huge potential in the photocatalytic field. Unfortunately, the photocatalytic performance of pristine Sb2WO6 is greatly confined by its low utilization efficiency of solar energy and high electron-holes recombination (Rafiq et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2020). Therefore, pristine Sb2WO6 can serve as a great substrate photocatalyst to couple with CQDs for constructing a novel visible to near-infrared responsive photocatalytic composite.

Herein, novel 0D/2D CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets were successfully fabricated by a one-step hydrothermal method. The composite exhibits superior photocatalytic degradation performance either under the visible light region or under NIR light region. However, pristine Sb2WO6 nearly has no catalytic activity under the illumination of NIR light. Moreover, the important roles of CQDs in the composite were deeply studied based on characterizations and DFT calculations. Deeper studies confirm that CQDs play dual roles in the composite. The first is the photoinduced electrons acceptor, which can effectively foster the separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs originating from Sb2WO6, and the second is its excellent up-converted property. Thus, this work not only proposes an avenue for synthesizing novel Vis-NIR responsive photocatalyst for the degradation of organic pollutants but also offers some reference with respect to theoretical simulation.

CQDs were fabricated by a simple hydrothermal method. Firstly, a mixture of 0.5 g urea and 1.0 g ascorbic acid was dissolved into 25 mL of deionized water and stirred continuously for 30 min. Then, the mixture was added to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, the mixture was filtered and centrifuged to remove larger particles, dialysis for two days to remove impurities. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain brown, black powder for further use.

Firstly, different amounts of CQDs (0.5%, 1% and 4% to the weight of Sb2WO6 in turn) were dispersed in deionized water. Subsequently, 1.37 g of SbCl3 and 0.76 g of (NH4)10W12O41·5H2O were dissolved in deionized water in turn, and the homogeneous solution was obtained after mixing evenly. The different concentrations of CQDs solutions were then mixed with the above solutions and stirred for 30 min. After that, the pH of the mixture solution was adjusted to 2 by 4 mol/L NaOH with continuous stirring and placed in an oven with the temperature turned to 180 ℃ for 48 h. The mixture solution was centrifuged, washed several times with water and ethanol, and dried to obtain dark-green powders. The CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets were denoted as 0.5% CQDs/Sb2WO6, 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6, and 4% CQDs/Sb2WO6, respectively. Additionally, pristine Sb2WO6 were synthesized referring to previous work without the addition of a CQDs solution (Bi et al., 2013).

The crystal structures of the as-prepared samples were characterized by a D/Max-B X-ray diffractometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analyses were carried out on a Nexus (Thermo Nicolet, U.S.A.). Raman spectra were recorded by a Raman spectrometer (InVia, Renishaw, Gloucestershire, U.K.). The morphologies of the samples were examined by scanning electronic microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-8010). The high-resolution images were obtained on a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, J.E.M. 2100F). The UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the catalysts were measured by an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (UV-4100). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were taken to detect the atomic surface concentration and element state on the catalysts by a PHI Quantera II system spectrometer. A fluorescence spectrometer (F-380) was carried out to determine the samples' photoluminescence spectra and time-dependent PL spectra. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and transient photocurrent were brought by an electrochemical workstation (CHI660C). The specific surface areas were measured on Autosorb-1 (Quantachrome Instruments) by the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometry was carried out to detect the active species on a Bruker ER200-SRC spectrometer. The intermediates of RhB degradation under visible light were measured via Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS, U.S.A.).

The photocatalytic performances of the samples were measured via the degradation of RhB (Rhodamine B) under visible light and NIR light irradiation. The experimental conditions are as follows: 0.1 g of the sample was placed in 100 mL of RhB aqueous solution with a concentration of 10 mg/L. In order to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and the dye mixture, the solution was stirred in the dark for 30 min before illumination at room temperature, and then the Xe lamp (Labsolar-IIIAG, Beijing Perfect Light Technology Co., Ltd.) with UV cutoff filter (λ>420 nm) and UV-Vis cutoff filter (λ>800 nm) were utilized as Vis and NIR light sources, respectively, to carry out the photocatalytic degradation experiment. At fixed time intervals (30 min for visible light and 60 min for NIR light), 5 mL of the suspension was centrifuged, and the absorbance was then tested by a UV–vis spectrometer at 554 nm. The experimental conditions for degradation of methyl orange (MO) and methylene blue (MB) were similar to those for degradation of RhB, with MO concentration of 10 mg/L and MB concentration of 20 mg/L. The absorbance of the solution after reaction was measured at 463 nm and 662 nm by UV–vis spectrometer, respectively.

In order to detect the signals of active substances in the photocatalytic degradation process of the CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets, the ESR spectra were performed under the following conditions: 30 mg composites were added into 1 mL of deionized water and 1 mL of methanol to detect •OH and •O2 −, respectively. Both of them used DMPO as a trapping agent.

Meanwhile, terephthalic acid (TA) was used as a fluorescent reagent to confirm the •OH radicals formed on the sample surface under visible and NIR irradiation, which can readily react with •OH radicals to produce high fluorescence 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (2-HTA). It is known that the peak intensity of 2-HTA is directly proportional to the amount of •OH radicals produced in the sample. The experiment conditions were as follows: 0.1 g 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets were suspended in 100 mL aqueous solution containing 0.16 g terephthalic acid and put under visible and NIR irradiation, respectively. A 5 mL aliquot was taken and then separated by centrifugation at fixed intervals during the experiment. Then the concentration of •OH radicals in the supernatant was analyzed using an F-380 fluorescence spectrometer at 315 nm excitation wavelength.

VASP (Vienna Ab-initio Simulation Package) model was used to simulate the electron properties of Sb2WO6 and CQDs/Sb2WO6 crystal structures. Before calculation, all crystal cells were optimized to reach the most stable structures. HES06 hybrid functional was utilized to calculate the DOS (density of states) and band structure of the Sb2WO6 crystal cell. The specific parameters of the density of states (DOS) and band structure of it are as follows: the precision is accurate. The electronic iteration convergence standard is 10−6 meV/cell. GGA-PBE functional was utilized to calculate the charge density difference CQDs/Sb2WO6 crystal cell. The parameters are as follows: the k-mesh was 2×1×1, and the precision is normal.

The morphologies and microstructures of the obtained composites were observed and analyzed under SEM, TEM, and HRTEM. Fig. S1a–d are SEM images of Sb2WO6, 0.5% CQDs/Sb2WO6, 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 and 4% CQDs/Sb2WO6, respectively. Similar to pristine Sb2WO6, CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets are still composed of a large number of thin-layer nanosheets with different shapes. Due to the limited resolution of SEM, the morphology of CQDs was further explored by TEM and HRTEM, and the distribution of CQDs on the Sb2WO6 surface was observed. As shown in Fig. 1a, CQDs are distributed homogeneously with a size range of about 4–7 nm, which can also be verified by the particle size distribution in Fig. 1b. The sharp contrast between Sb2WO6 and CQDs in Fig. 1c confirms that CQDs are evenly distributed on the surface of Sb2WO6 nanosheets. Fig. 1d shows the HRTEM image of 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6. Clearly, the lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.23 nm can be observed distinctly, which corresponds to (2−11) crystal plane of pristine Sb2WO6 (Fig. 1e). The SAED pattern depicts a bright concentric diffraction ring, revealing the polycrystalline nature of pristine Sb2WO6 (Fig. 1f). In addition, the EDS (element distribution mappings) show that O, Sb, W and C elements were uniformly dispersed on the surface of the 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets (Fig. 1(h–k)). These results further suggested that CQDs are successfully introduced onto the surface of Sb2WO6 nanosheets.

Fig. 2a is the XRD pattern of the as-prepared composites. The diffraction peaks of 20.4°, 26.90°, 32.83°, 36.37°, 40.25°, 47.49°, 49.69° and 53.07° in the figure can correspond to the characteristic peaks of triclinic phase Sb2WO6 (JCPDS No. 47-1680) (Yang et al., 2016). Moreover, the characteristic diffraction peaks of CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets have no obvious shift, which indicates that the CQDs are not incorporated into the Sb2WO6 lattice. Therefore, it can be preliminarily inferred that CQDs are loaded on the surface of Sb2WO6. The XRD patterns of all the CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets are similar to pristine Sb2WO6. No obvious peaks attributed to CQDs are detected, which are probably because of the low contents of CQDs introduced into the composites. In order to investigate the functional groups and surface chemical bonds of the CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets, FT-IR spectroscopy was recorded and shown in Fig. 2b. As shown, the two absorption bands at about 1637 and 3435 cm−1 are attributed to the physically adsorbed water, while the absorption band located at 442–721 cm−1 owing to the stretching vibrations of Sb–O, W–O, and W–O–W bonds (Rafiq and Majid, 2019). For pristine CQDs, the absorption bands near 3441 and 1425 cm−1 can be attributed to the tensile vibration and in-plane bending vibration of –OH, respectively. In addition, the absorption bands near 1672 and 1034 cm−1 indicate the presence of CO and C–O vibrations, respectively (Chen and Fan, 2017; Hasija et al., 2019). The above results indicate that there are many CO, C–O and –OH groups on the surface of the synthesized CQDs. However, no significant characteristic peak of CQDs was detected in FT-IR spectra of CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets, indicating a low content of CQDs in the composite.

The existence of CQDs in CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets can be further verified by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S2，the characteristic peaks of pristine Sb2WO6 can be observed at about 245, 300, 423, 500, 760, and 880 cm−1 (Rafiq and Majid, 2019). Moreover, the two typical characteristic peaks, located at 1603 cm−1 and 1361 cm−1, were characteristic peaks for CQDs, which could be assigned to sp2 (G band) and sp3 (D band) hybridized carbons, respectively (Yu et al., 2014). Obviously, the typical characteristic peaks of Sb2WO6 and CQDs simultaneously appeared in the Raman patterns of the 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets, indicating that CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets are successfully synthesized.

The surface chemical composition and valence states of the synthesized samples were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. S3 shows the full survey of Sb2WO6 and 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6, where Sb, O, W, and C are detected in the photocatalysts. The peak at 284.8 eV is due to the XPS instrument itself possessing carbon-hydrogen. Besides, the high-resolution XPS spectrums of the above elements are shown in Fig. 3a–d. The Sb 3d signals in Sb2WO6 can be fitted to two peaks with binding energies of 539.8 eV and 530.5 eV, corresponding to Sb 3d3/2 and Sb 3d5/2, respectively (Fig. 3a) (Chen et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 3b, the peak at 530.5 eV observed in the O 1s spectrum cannot be attributed in detail because the banding energy of Sb 3d and O 1s overlapped and interweaved from 526 to 536 eV. The W 4f spectra of Sb2WO6 can be fitted into four peaks with binding energies of 37.8 eV, 36.0 eV, 35.6 eV and 34.4 eV, respectively, which are attributed to W6+ 4f5/2, W5+ 4f5/2, W6+ 4f7/2 and W5+ 4f7/2 (Fig. 3c). The fractions of W6+ and W5+ (Table. S1) are 54.25% and 45.75%, respectively(Wang et al., 2018). It is worth noting that, compared with pristine Sb2WO6, the peaks of Sb, O, and W of 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets samples show redshifts after the introduction of CQDs, indicating that electrons on the Sb2WO6 side tend to transfer to the CQDs side due to the electron screening effect (Wang et al., 2021a). In addition, as shown in Table S1, the proportion of W5+ in 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 was higher than that in Sb2WO6. This indicates that part of W6+ atoms in Sb2WO6 are reduced to W5+ by the introduced CQDs during the synthesis process. Besides, three peaks at 284.8, 286.1, and 286.5 eV are observed in the C 1s spectrum, corresponding to the C-C bond and oxygen-containing carbon of CQDs, which means the successful introduction of CQDs (Fig. 3d) (Di et al., 2016a; He et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019a).

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the composites was conducted to measure the thermal stability. According to Fig. 3e, the TGA curves of 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 show two steps to weight loss. The initial weight loss in the range of 50–220 °C is due to the adsorbed water molecules in the structure. The other step is attributed to the removal of surface functional groups and the oxidation of CQDs (Wang et al., 2020). The TGA curves of pristine Sb2WO6 and the composites are compared in Fig. 3f. It can be observed that in air atmosphere, with the increase of temperature, the weight of the sample first decreases and then increases, which may be due to the existing oxygen vacancies in the sample.

The specific surface areas and BJH pore size distribution curves of a series of CQDs/Sb2WO6 samples were studied by nitrogen adsorption desorption isotherm analysis. The results are shown in Fig. S4. The adsorption desorption isotherms of all samples are type IV isotherms with H3-type hysteresis loops, indicating the existence of mesopores (size 2–50 nm), which is likely due to the aggregation of nanosheets. In addition, the BET specific surface area, pore volume and average pore diameter of the prepared samples are summarized in Table. S2. Compared with pure antimony tungstate, CQDs/Sb2WO6 composites prepared by introducing CQDs into Sb2WO6 have higher specific surface area and pore volume. It is worth noting that higher BET surface area can provide more active sites and absorb more active species, which is conducive to the degradation of pollutants.

RhB dye was selected as the target organic pollutant to appraise the photocatalytic performance of the different photocatalysts. For pristine Sb2WO6, its photocatalytic degradation efficiency only reaches 8% in 120 min under visible light irradiation ( Fig. 4a). In contrast, the degradation efficiency of RhB over CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets was significantly improved. Especially for 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6, it has the highest photocatalytic degradation efficiency for RhB, reaching 83% in 120 min. The apparent first-order rate constants (k) of various photocatalysts are described in Fig. 4b. Obviously, the k values of 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 are about 7 times higher than that of pristine Sb2WO6. Considering the up-converted property of CQDs, the near-infrared photocatalytic activity of CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets have also been investigated (Fig. 4c). Similarly, 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets still keep the highest photocatalytic degradation efficiency and k value (0.00210 min−1). Moreover, in comparison with other SbWO3-based photocatalyst, 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets also display an excellent activity, as listed in Table. S3. It is worth mentioning that excess CQDs (>4.0 wt%) lead to a noticeable decrease of photocatalytic activity. This phenomenon may be ascribed to the high content of CQDs around the composite can lead to a competition for absorption of photons. The excitation energy or emission energy will be absorbed by the CQDs, while the energy reaching the CQDs/Sb2WO6 composite material is low, resulting an inner filter effect. In addition, excessive CQDs will accumulate on the surface and pores of the composites, thus affecting the adsorption of pollutants (Pan et al., 2014).

In order to prove the practicability of the composites for the degradation of other dye pollutants, the degradation of MO and MB was tested under visible light. It can be seen from Figs. S5a and c that the concentration changes of MO and MB are tiny without catalyst, indicating that both dyes are stable under Vis light irradiation. Similarly, compared with the original Sb2WO6, the degradation effects of the composites on MO and MB are still significantly improved. Among them, the degradation efficiency of the optimal ratio composite 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets is the highest, and the removal rates of MO and MB under the same conditions are 93% and 95% respectively. The results show that the prepared 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 catalyst has good catalytic efficiency. TOC (Total organic carbon) analysis was conducted to explore the mineralization rate of organic dyes. The change of TOC in RhB, MO and MB solutions using 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 as catalyst was described in Fig. S5e. After different degradation time (Vis: 2 h, NIR: 4 h), compared with the original dye solution, the TOC concentration of the final solution decreased to different degrees. Although not completely mineralized, most of the dye molecules have been oxidized into small molecule intermediates. The above results indicated that the introduction of CQDs could effectively enhance the photocatalytic activity.

In addition, we further explored the influence of different experimental conditions on RhB degradation efficiency over 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6. As shown in Fig. S6a-b, with the catalyst dosage increasing from 0.05 g to 0.1 g, the degradation efficiency of RhB over the optimal sample is both improved. However, when the dosage is further increased to 0.2 g/L, the degradation efficiency does not significantly improve, and the final degradation efficiency is basically the same as that of 0.1 g/L. It is well known that the initial pH of a solution affects the ionic properties of dye molecules and the surface charges of photocatalysts, and thus the interfacial interactions. Therefore, it is crucial to choose the appropriate pH value to study the degradation of dyes by photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. S6c, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of RhB is usually increased when the initial pH of the solution is lower than 7. This may be due to the weak dissociation of carboxyl groups in RhB molecules under acidic conditions, which reduces the electrostatic repulsion between the catalyst and the dye molecules, thus increasing the adsorption amount of RhB and the degradation rate of the catalyst (Li et al., 2018). However, a further decrease in pH will inhibit the degradation of the dye, probably because the increased molecular weight of RhB adsorbed on the catalyst surface will be detrimental to light penetration (Natarajan et al., 2011).

In addition to the initial pH of solution, the effects of abundant inorganic anions (Cl-, HCO3 -, NO3 -, SO4 2−) and DOM (HA, representing DOM in this study) in dye wastewater on the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of composite materials are also worthy of further study. As shown in Fig. S6e, it can be found that the added inorganic anions all inhibit the photocatalytic process of RhB dye, and the inhibition degree is in the order of HCO3 ->SO4 2−>NO3 ->Cl-. This inhibition may come from the following two reasons: (1) Competitive adsorption of these inorganic anions and target pollutant molecules on the surface of the catalyst leads to blockage of the active site on the surface of the photocatalyst, thus inhibiting the degradation of pollutant molecules. (2) In addition, due to their low oxidation potential, these negatively charged inorganic anions can react with holes (h+) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) as free radical scavengers to generate inorganic free radicals with low activity, which greatly hinders the photocatalytic degradation reaction (Govarthanan et al., 2021).

By exploring the influence of co-existing DOM on RhB degradation effect, it can be seen that in CQDs/Sb2WO6 system, DOM concentration of different concentrations can promote RhB photodegradation process. When DOM concentration increased from 0 mg/L to 5 mg/L, 10 mg/L and 20 mg/L, RhB degradation efficiency varied from 83% to 97%, 99% and 83% at 2 h. This is because humic acid is a photosensitive organic matter, which is easy to be excited to produce a series of active intermediates such as superoxide free radicals and hydroxyl free radicals, thus promoting the photodegradation of organic pollutants (Li et al., 2019a). When the DOM concentration increases to 20 mg/L, the promotion effect of DOM on RhB degradation is no longer obvious, which may be because DOM occupies the active site of the composite material and inhibits the adsorption of RhB molecule on the surface of the composite material, leading to the decrease of photocatalytic activity (Choi et al., 2014).

The photocatalytic stability of the optimal sample under Vis and NIR irradiation was investigated via cycle experiments. As shown in Figs. 4e and f, after five times utilization, the photocatalytic activity of the optimal sample under the two conditions hardly decreased, which indicates the composite has good photocatalytic stability. In addition, the best sample was compared with fresh ones by XRD, SEM and XPS. As shown in Fig. S7a, the crystal form of the sample did not change significantly after the reaction, and the used 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 still maintained a complete crystal structure. By comparing the SEM spectra, it can be seen that there is no obvious difference in the apparent structure of 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 after cyclic reaction, and the physical structure is relatively stable (Fig. S7b). Finally, the chemical structure stability of the composites was analyzed by comparing the XPS spectra of the composites before and after cycling. There was no significant difference in Sb 3d and W 4f spectra of the samples before and after use, which further verified the superior stability of the best samples (Figs. S7c and d). Considering the toxicity of antimony, we measured the leaching concentration of the composites after visible light irradiation for 2 h, 4 h and 6 h. The results are shown in Fig. S8. After visible light irradiation for 2 h, 4 h and 6 h, the leaching antimony concentrations were 0.2629 mg/L, 0.2931 mg/L and 0.2641 mg/L, respectively. Interestingly, the concentration of leched sb did not increase with the increase of irradiation time, indicating that the leached sb element was not continuously leached from the catalyst. In addition, according to the emission standard (GB 30770-2014, China), the total antimony emission limit is 0.3 mg/L. In this study, the concentration of antimony leached is lower than this value, indicating that antimony leached from photocatalyst is hardly harmful to water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and photoelectrochemical properties of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para:
A series of optical and photoelectrochemical tests were carried out to investigate why the composites exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance for RhB removal under visible and NIR light, respectively. It can be obviously seen in Fig. 5a that compared with pristine Sb2WO6, the absorption edge of the CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets significantly appear redshift, and the absorption edge range is extended from 485 nm to 517 nm. Moreover, the degree of redshift seems to be linear with the amount of CQDs in the composites. In order to further determine the band gap of the samples, we plotted via the Taucplot calculated from the DRS data. The bandgap energy of Sb2WO6 was reckoned as 2.37 eV (Fig. S9). In order to determine the semiconductor type and flat band potential of Sb2WO6, the Mott-Schottky curve was recorded and analyzed in Fig. 5b. It can be observed that the slope of the Mott-Schottky curve has a positive slope, suggesting that pristine Sb2WO6 is an n-type semiconductor (Cao et al., 2018). Meanwhile, the flat band potential of Sb2WO6 is determined to be −0.45 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl). The Ag/AgCl electrode potential is converted to normal hydrogen electrode potential (NHE) according to the following equation (Shi et al., 2020):
E
NHE
= E ( Ag / AgCl ) −
E
θ
+ 0.059 pH

When Eθ (Ag/AgCl, at pH=7)=0.197 V, the flat band potential in Fig. 5b is −0.24 V vs. NHE. Owing to the conduction band potential (ECB) of n-type semiconductor is approximately equal to the flat band potential, the valence band potential of Sb2WO6 can be determined to be 2.13 V vs. NHE. Fig. 5c presents the PL spectra of pristine Sb2WO6, 0.5% CQDs/Sb2WO6, 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 and 4% CQDs/Sb2WO6. Among all the composites, 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 shows the obvious lowest PL signal, which indicates that it can effectively reduce the recombination efficiency of photogenerated carriers (Di et al., 2015a, 2015b; Liu et al., 2021).

The transient photocurrent responses of as-prepared catalysts under visible light and NIR light were monitored and displayed in Fig. 5d and e, respectively. Obviously, the photocurrent of 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 under both light conditions is significantly higher than that of pristine Sb2WO6. In addition, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) show that the diameters of arc radii of 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 are clearly much smaller than that of pristine Sb2WO6 (Fig. 5f). The above results indicate that the introduction of CQDs can speed up the migration and separation of photogenerated carriers, which greatly improves the photocatalytic performance of the original Sb2WO6 (Gao et al., 2019; Xie et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021b). Furthermore, CQDs are confirmed to serve as sinks to capture electrons and lead to more effective separation of electron–hole charges (Qin and Zeng, 2017).

The above researches indicated that the Sb2WO6 sample compounded with CQDs exhibited nice photocatalytic ability and generated certain photocurrent response under NIR light, which may be due to the up-conversion effect of CQDs. According to previous reports on CQDs, photon up-conversion is an anti-Stokes emission process (Jiang et al., 2021a, 2021b). When electrons in a carbon quantum dot π orbital are excited by a low-energy photon, the photogenic electron will move to a higher energy state. The excited electrons can then fall back to σ orbits via radiation relaxation, emitting light at shorter wavelengths to excite the surrounding semiconductors (Miao et al., 2016). This provides a solid foundation for the design of near-infrared photocatalysts. The photoluminescence spectral record of the as-prepared CQDs in water is shown in Fig. 6b. Obviously, it is found that CQDs can convert the long-wavelength light from 550 to 850 nm to the short-wavelength light from 440 to 500 nm, which can be subsequently absorbed by Sb2WO6 to generate active electron–hole pairs (Ke et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019c; Wei et al., 2019). This characteristic can be attributed to the multi-photon activity process, Which means that layered Sb2WO6 can realize the indirect utilization of NIR by utilizing the fluorescence effect of CQDs, thereby enhancing the light absorption response range (Yang et al., 2018). In addition, the optical images of CQDs irradiated by white light and ultraviolet light (365 nm) are shown in Fig. 6a. Quantum dots have strong ultraviolet fluorescence, which can be clearly seen by the naked eye.

The DFT calculations were implemented to furtherly investigate the electron properties of Sb2WO6 and the electron transfer tendency at the interface between CQDs and Sb2WO6. Fig. S10 is the optimized primitive crystal structure of Sb2WO6. Fig. 6c is the density of states (DOS) of Sb2WO6, which indicates the valence band maximum (VBM) of Sb2WO6 is mainly composed of O 2s orbital while the conduction band minimum (CBM) of Sb2WO6 is the constitution of hybrid orbitals of W 5d and O 2s. The DOS result demonstrates that the photoinduced electrons may originate from the O 2s orbital of Sb2WO6 and can then be excited about the hybrid orbitals of W 5d and O 2s. The calculated bandgap value of Sb2WO6 is 2.23 eV, which is very close to the experimental value (2.37 eV). Moreover, Sb2WO6 is a direct transition semiconductor photocatalyst, consistent with the experimental result in Figs. S9. S11a and S11b are CQDs-Sb2WO6 crystal structures before optimization and after optimization, respectively. It is clear that CQDs are inclined to move to the Sb2WO6 side while O atoms in Sb2WO6 are prone to escaping to the CQDs side, suggesting a strong interfacial interaction exists between CQDs and Sb2WO6. Fig. 6e depicts the charge density difference at the interface between CQDs and Sb2WO6. It is obvious that C atoms (cyan color region) grasp rich electrons from O atoms (yellow color region), which can be further demonstrated by the planar-averaged charge density difference (the positive value represents electron accumulation region, and the negative value represents electron depletion region) in Fig. 6f. Based on the above analyses of DFT calculations, a detailed electron transfer path can be as follows: electrons originating from O 2s orbital of Sb2WO6 are firstly excited to the hybrid orbitals of W 5d and O 2s. Subsequently, the photoinduced electrons can mitigate from O 2s orbital to C 2s orbital of CQDs and then participate in the next photocatalytic reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism and pathway of the photocatalytic reaction process

ce:section.ce:para: The trapping experiments were carried out to elucidate the contributions of active species to RhB degradation, as shown in Fig. 7a. Typically, sodium oxalate (AO), isopropanol (IPA), and benzoquinone (BQ) are used as scavengers to remove the photogenerated holes (h+), •OH and •O2 − (Di et al., 2016b; Wang et al., 2019b; Xiao et al., 2019). Obviously, after the addition of AO and IPA, the degradation efficiency of RhB is remarkably decreased to 55.1% and 71.2%, respectively, which demonstrates that h+ and •OH play critical roles in the degradation of the RhB over the optimal sample. Furthermore, BQ has no obvious inhibition role to the photocatalytic activity, which suggests the composite can not induce the production of •O2 −. In order to more intuitively observe •O2 − and •OH, the ESR technique was carried out. The characteristic signals of DMPO-hydroxyl radical (•OH) produced by the optimal sample and pristine Sb2WO6 are displayed in Fig. 7b. Distinctly, the optimal sample exhibits much stronger signals than that of Sb2WO6 under the same conditions, indicating that the CQDs modified Sb2WO6 had higher •OH production and photogenerated carrier utilization under irradiation. In addition, the characteristic signals of the DMPO-superoxide radical (•O2 −) could not be seen in Fig. S12.

Because the •OH is considered to be the main reactive species causing degradation, the photocatalytic generation of •OH on the composites was determined by the terephthalic acid photoluminescence probing technique (TA-PL). The center of the fluorescence signal peak (426 nm) of 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid can be observed in Figs. 7c and d, which can be used to characterize the existence of •OH in the system (González-Casamachin et al., 2019; Qi et al., 2019). It can be observed that the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with increased illumination time in all cases of the catalytic processes, implying the radical •OH radicals are continuously generated in the reaction system (Waiskopf et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019d). Furthermore, the 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets show stronger PL peak intensity than pristine Sb2WO6 due to improved charge separation, which means that the photocatalytic activity of Sb2WO6 is enhanced after introducing CQDs. These are in accordance with the previous analysis Fig. 8.

The possible degradation pathway of RhB was also explored. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of RhB under visible and NIR irradiation were recorded in Fig. S13a and b, respectively. It can be observed that the intensity of the characteristic peak near 554 nm decreases with the increase of irradiation time either under visible light irradiation or under NIR light irradiation. As is well known, the degradation of RhB structure and the reduction of absorption band without a wavelength shift indicated that the degradation pathway might be the cleavage of conjugated chromophore structure of RhB (Zhang et al., 2019b). In summary, the decrease of RhB concentration and the UV-Vis absorption spectra confirm the efficient degradation of RhB by the optimal sample.

Furthermore, the intermediates in the 1% CQDs/Sb2WO6-RhB reaction system were analyzed by LC-MS (Fig. S14). It is found that mass-to-charge (m/z) ratios are mainly at about 424, 218, 130, 103, 102, 84, 73, 61 and 59 after the reaction goes on for 60 min, which indicates that the RhB molecules (m = 443) can be degraded and produce some intermediates. These results demonstrate that the intermediates of macromolecules can eventually be degraded into small molecules, and the cleavage of chromophore structure predominated in the reaction system, accompanied by a small amount of de monoethyl reaction (Wang et al., 2021a). The possible degradation pathways of RhB are shown in Fig. 7e. Firstly, various reactive species such as h+ and •OH attacked the central carbon of RhB to degrade the dye via N-deethylation and chromophore cleavage processes to yield intermediates. Then, further degradation makes intermediates with lower molecular weight are generated by ring-opening reactions. Ultimately, these intermediates are gradually decomposed into CO2 and H2O.

Similarly, the mineralization pathways of MO and MB were further studied by LC-MS instrument, and the results are shown in Fig. S15 and S16, respectively. According to the species detected in this study and the previous literature, the main degradation pathways of MO can be inferred as follows: since various active free radicals (mainly h+ and •OH) preferentially attack MO molecules, the parent MO forms P2, P3 and P4 respectively due to the loss of alkyl, hydroxyl and desulfurization (Govindan et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2020). After a series of azo bond breaking, sulfonic acid group removal and continuous demethylation reactions, these intermediates are further broken into small molecules through different ways, and CO2 and H2O are produced finally (Bahrudin and Nawi, 2019). According to the LC-MS test results, the possible degradation pathway of MB was proposed as shown in Fig. S16. Due to the presence of high-energy electrons, MB molecules are converted by demethylation into fragments at peaks m/z=279, 256, 217 (Liu et al., 2017; Wahab et al., 2011). Meanwhile, previous studies showed that the benzene ring of MB could form fragments corresponding to m/z=362 and 338 due to hydroxylation reaction. In addition to some of the low bond energy products observed, possible intermediates of MB decomposition include ionic products such as acetate, oxalate and sulfate. Further mineralization of these products results in the formation of CO2, H2O, SO4 2- and NO3 -.

The unique up-conversion property of CQDs induces near-infrared photocatalysis, which makes the semiconductor materials without infrared effect exhibit good NIR photocatalytic degradation effect. Therefore, the possible reaction mechanisms for the degradation of RhB by CQDs/Sb2WO6 photocatalysis system under visible light and NIR light were proposed based on the above-discussed results. First of all, the electron-hole pairs are generated when the composite material is irradiated by short-wavelength light with high-energy photons. Meanwhile, when CQDs/Sb2WO6 is irradiated by long-wavelength light, the CQDs act as spectral converters to absorb the long-wavelength light and emit shorter-wavelength light through its up-conversion property, thus expanding the light response range of pristine Sb2WO6. Secondly, when the electrons are excited from the valence band of Sb2WO6 to the conduction band, they can be collected by CQDs, thus extending the lifetime of electrons. Subsequently, holes remaining in the valence band reacted with H2O to generate radical •OH, mainly reactive radical species during the photocatalytic process. These •OH radicals will attack molecules of pollutants, decompose them into various intermediates products, and eventually convert them into CO2 and H2O. Therefore, the CQDs can not only significantly improve the solar energy utilization rate of CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets through their unique up-conversion characteristics, but also can act as the acceptant of photogenerated electrons, effectively improve the electron transfer in the photocatalytic system.

A novel Vis-NIR light-responsive CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets were successfully fabricated. In comparison with pristine Sb2WO6, the optimal CQDs/Sb2WO6 nanosheets possess remarkably enhanced photocatalytic degradation performance under visible and near-infrared light. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of the composite is mainly attributed to the dual roles of CQDs in the composite, which can not only promote the separation of photogenerated charge carriers but also convert long-wavelength from 550 to 850 nm to short-wavelength light from 440 to 500 nm. Besides, DFT calculations reveal that an ultrafast electron transfer tunnel exists at the interlayer between CQDs-Sb2WO6 and electrons at O 2s orbital of Sb2WO6 can mitigate to the hybrid orbitals of W 5d and O 2s, and then the photoinduced electrons will transfer to the C 2s orbital of CQDs. Trapping experiments and the ESR technique validate h+ and •OH play major roles in photocatalytic reactions. This work discloses the possibility of utilizing the up-conversion role of CQDs to obtain highly efficient Vis-NIR responsive photocatalysts for wastewater purification.

Wenxuan Li: Experiment, Writing-Original draft preparation. Zhuangzhuang Wang: Data processing & analysis, Writing-review & editing. Yuan Li: Validation, Writing-review & editing. Jahan B Ghasemi: Data analysis, Discussion. Jun Li: Methodology, Software. Gaoke Zhang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.
